coredata.dc:title: Mechanically strong Janus tri-bore hollow fiber membranes with asymmetric pores for anti-wetting and anti-fouling membrane distillation
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yuanmiaoliang
Lu, Kang-Jia
Liang, Can Zeng
Chung, Tai-Shung
coredata.dc:description: Wetting, fouling and mechanical robustness are three major challenges constraining the commercial applications of membrane distillation (MD). Herein, we have developed a robust Janus tri-bore hollow fiber membrane with anti-wetting/fouling resistance. The unique membrane was fabricated by an oxidant-induced deposition of polydopamine/polyethylenimine (PDA/PEI) and a subsequent grafting of sodium-functionalized carbon quantum dots (Na+-CQDs) on top of a hydrophobic tri-bore polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) hollow fiber membrane. The PDA/PEI deposition resulted in a nanoporous top layer, and the Na+-CQD grafting significantly enhanced the membrane surface hydrophilicity. In MD operations, the nanoporous PDA/PEI layer endowed the membrane with an excellent rejection to surfactants, thus reducing the wetting propensity, while the grafted Na+-CQDs facilitated the formation of a surface hydration layer, thereby mitigating the oil fouling. This study may expand the applications of mechanically robust tri-bore MD hollow fiber membranes to desalinate hypersaline and harsh wastewaters containing amphiphilic and hydrophobic contaminants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Membrane distillation
Multi-bore hollow fiber membrane
Size exclusion
Surface hydration
Anti-wetting
Anti-fouling

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of tri-bore PVDF hollow fiber membranes
Fabrication of Janus tri-bore hollow fiber membranes consisting of hydrophobic micropores and hydrophilic nanopores
Desalination of a simulated multi-component wastewater
Properties of tri-bore PVDF hollow fiber membranes
Thickness and pore size of the PDA/PEI layer
Desalination performance with a simulated wastewater

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National University of Singapore Graduate School for Integrative Sciences & Engineering Scholarship

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Yuanmiaoliang Chen is grateful to the National University of Singapore Graduate School for Integrative Sciences & Engineering Scholarship (NGSS). Special thanks to Mr. Chuning Shang for the PAS measurements and Dr. Wenxiao Gai for the CQD synthesis. Prof Chung also likes to thank the Yushan Scholar Program supported by the Ministry of Education, Taiwan.

Yuanmiaoliang Chen is grateful to the National University of Singapore Graduate School for Integrative Sciences & Engineering Scholarship (NGSS). Special thanks to Mr. Chuning Shang for the PAS measurements and Dr. Wenxiao Gai for the CQD synthesis. Prof Chung also likes to thank the Yushan Scholar Program supported by the Ministry of Education, Taiwan.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fabrication of membranes. (a) Schematic design of the tri-bore spinneret. (b) The TEM image and size distribution of Na+-CQDs. (c) Fabrication scheme of a PVDF-Px-CQD membrane.

Morphology of membranes. FESEM images of outer surfaces of (a) PVDF, (b) PVDF-P0.5, (c) PVDF-P1, and (d) PVDF-P1-CQD membranes. (e-h) The corresponding cross-sections. The insets show the enlarged local morphology.

Thickness and pore size of the PDA/PEI layer (a) R parameter as a function of positron incident energy (bottom X axis) and depth into a membrane (top X axis). The dash lines indicate the thicknesses of the PDA/PEI layers of PVDF-P0.5 and PVDF-P1 membranes. (b) Pore size distribution of the PVDF membrane. (c, d) Rejections of polyethylene glycols (PEGs) with different molecular weights for PVDF-P0.5 and PVDF-P1 membranes. The insets are the pore diameter distributions derived from the rejection curves.
Characteristics of the outer membrane surfaces. (a) FTIR spectra, (b) XPS spectra, and (c) water contact angles (WCAs).

Wetting resistance of membranes. Normalized vapor flux (blue circles, left Y axis) and salt rejection (red squares, right Y axis) of (a) PVDF, (b) PVDF-P0.5, and (c) PVDF-P1 membranes. The vapor fluxes were normalized by the initial fluxes, which were 18.3, 15.9, and 12.4 Lm-2h−1 for the PVDF, PVDF-P0.5, and PVDF-P1 membranes, respectively. A 0.6 M NaCl solution and DI water were initially used as the feed and the distillate, respectively. The dash lines represent the additions of SDS in the feed solution. (d) Schematic illustrations of the wetting behaviors of the PVDF (left) and PVDF-P1 (right) membranes.

Fouling resistance of membranes. Normalized vapor flux (blue circles, left Y axis) and salt rejection (red squares, right Y axis) of (a) PVDF, (b) PVDF-P1, and (c) PVDF-P1-CQD membranes. The vapor fluxes were normalized by the initial fluxes, which were 17.9, 11.8, and 12.7 Lm-2h−1 for the PVDF, PVDF-P1, and PVDF-P1-CQD membranes, respectively. An oil-in-water saline solution (0.6 M NaCl, 1 g/L crude oil, 0.03 g/L SDS) and DI water were used as the feed and the distillate, respectively. (d) Schematic illustrations of the fouling behaviors of the PVDF (left) and PVDF-P1-CQD (right) membranes.

Desalination performance with a simulated wastewater. Normalized vapor flux (top) and distillate conductivity (bottom) of PVDF and PVDF-P1-CQD membranes as a function of water recovery. The vapor fluxes were normalized by the initial fluxes, which were 18.6 and 12.2 Lm-2h−1 for the PVDF and PVDF-P1-CQD membranes, respectively. A simulated wastewater and DI water were used as the feed and the distillate, respectively. The chemical composition of the simulated wastewater is presented in Table S2.
Comparison of dimensions, mechanical properties, LEPs, and MD fluxes of tri-bore PVDF hollow fiber membranes as a function of spinning conditions. a.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Fluxes of membranes were obtained in MD operations using a 0.6 M NaCl solution and DI water as the feed and distillate, respectively. Experimental parameters: the feed and distillate flow rates were 0.3 L/min and 0.1 L/min, respectively; the feed and distillate temperatures were 60 and 20 °C, respectively. The salt rejections of all the membranes were over 99.9 %.

head.ce:title: Mechanically strong Janus tri-bore hollow fiber membranes with asymmetric pores for anti-wetting and anti-fouling membrane distillation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: NUS Graduate School for Integrative Science and Engineering

National Taiwan University of Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, National University of Singapore, Singapore 117585, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Wetting, fouling and mechanical robustness are three major challenges constraining the commercial applications of membrane distillation (MD). Herein, we have developed a robust Janus tri-bore hollow fiber membrane with anti-wetting/fouling resistance. The unique membrane was fabricated by an oxidant-induced deposition of polydopamine/polyethylenimine (PDA/PEI) and a subsequent grafting of sodium-functionalized carbon quantum dots (Na+-CQDs) on top of a hydrophobic tri-bore polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) hollow fiber membrane. The PDA/PEI deposition resulted in a nanoporous top layer, and the Na+-CQD grafting significantly enhanced the membrane surface hydrophilicity. In MD operations, the nanoporous PDA/PEI layer endowed the membrane with an excellent rejection to surfactants, thus reducing the wetting propensity, while the grafted Na+-CQDs facilitated the formation of a surface hydration layer, thereby mitigating the oil fouling. This study may expand the applications of mechanically robust tri-bore MD hollow fiber membranes to desalinate hypersaline and harsh wastewaters containing amphiphilic and hydrophobic contaminants.

ce:section.ce:para: Membrane distillation (MD) is an emerging membrane-based thermal desalination process that relies on a microporous and hydrophobic membrane [1].Comparing to those pressure-driven membrane technologies such as reverse osmosis (RO) and nanofiltration (NF), MD requires a much lower capital cost because its operation systems do not involve high-pressure components [2]. In addition, its moderate working temperature enables it to harvest low-grade thermal energy including solar and geothermal energy and industrial waste heat, which can considerately reduce not only the energy cost but also the carbon footprint [3,4]. Besides, the high tolerance of MD to salinity makes it desirable to treat hypersaline brines such as shale gas wastewater and brines generated in zero liquid discharge (ZLD) processes [5–7].

Membranes are the key component in MD processes. Flat sheet and hollow fiber membranes are the most common configurations in laboratorial and industrial MD systems [8]. The former is advantageous for its ease of fabrication and assembly. However, it is inferior to its hollow fiber counterpart due to the lower surface-area-to-volume ratio [9,10]. Moreover, compared to flat sheet membrane modules, the footprint required to install hollow fiber membrane modules is significantly reduced [11,12]. Therefore, the hollow fiber configuration has greater potential in real world applications. Nevertheless, the mechanical properties of typical single-bore hollow fiber membranes need to be further improved in order for them to be safety adopted in long-term and large-scale MD operations. To achieve it, robust multi-bore hollow fiber membranes with various geometries, including lotus-root-like seven-bore and blossom-like tri-bore, have been developed [13,14]. Owing to the spokes bridging the bore channels, multi-bore hollow fiber membranes generally exhibit superior mechanical properties to single-bore hollow fibers in both radical and axial directions. In addition, multi-bore hollow fiber membranes possess other advantages such as high porosity, good wetting resistance, and ease of module fabrication [15]. Nonetheless, similar to the conventional hydrophobic MD membranes, the applications of current multi-bore hollow fiber membranes are still limited to relatively “clean” feed solutions to minimize wetting and fouling issues [13,14,16].

Membrane wetting is a prominent challenge in MD where the feed liquid freely permeates through the membrane pores, resulting in a compromised salt rejection and contaminated distillate [17]. Hydrophobic MD membranes are susceptible to membrane wetting when the feed solution contains surfactants or low-surface-tension contaminants [18,19]. Fouling is another challenge where contaminants are accumulated on membrane surfaces or inside the pores [20–22]. Thus, the pathways for vapor transport are blocked and the vapor flux is diminished. In particular, conventional hydrophobic MD membranes are especially prone to fouling by hydrophobic contaminants owing to the strong hydrophobic-hydrophobic interaction between the membranes and the foulants [23,24].

To address the wetting and fouling issues, tremendous efforts have been made to develop novel MD membranes with special materials and architectures. For example, omniphobic membranes, where the intrusion of liquids is energetically unfavored, remain a prevailing method to tackle membrane wetting [25–28]. However, the fabrication of re-entrance structures for omniphobic membranes is complicated and time-consuming. Recently, Chen et al. developed a novel wetting mitigation strategy by constructing a nanoporous layer on conventional MD membranes [29]. The nanoporous dense layer could reject surfactant molecules by size exclusion and thus avoid pore wetting. Furthermore, the oil-induced fouling was also mitigated if enhancing the hydrophilicity of the nanoporous layer because its underwater-oleophobic nature impedes the oil attachment during MD operations [30]. Despite the tremendous efforts, by far, most membranes with simultaneous anti-wetting and anti-fouling properties are in the configurations of flat sheets and single-bore hollow fibers. To the best of our knowledge, no multi-bore hollow fiber MD membranes with sufficient wetting and fouling resistance have been reported.

In this study, Janus tri-bore hollow fiber membranes consisting of hydrophobic micropores and hydrophilic nanopores have been developed to simultaneously achieve superior mechanical stability, wetting and fouling resistances. A tri-bore hollow fiber substrate made of polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) was first prepared by a dry-jet wet-spinning phase inversion method. Then, the Janus hollow fiber membranes were fabricated by depositing a compact and robust polydopamine/polyethylenimine (PDA/PEI) layer and subsequently grafting sodium-functionalized carbon quantum dots (Na+-CQDs) on the microporous PVDF substrate. Direct contact MD (DCMD) wetting and fouling experiments were conducted to investigate the wetting and fouling resistance of the newly developed membranes using a surfactant and oil as the wetting and fouling agents, respectively. Finally, by relating the structural and chemical properties of the membranes to their wetting and fouling behaviors, the underlying science of anti-wetting and anti-fouling mechanisms was revealed and elucidated.

The PVDF Kynar HSV900 resin was acquired from Arkema Inc. N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, > 99.5%), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.5%), and isopropanol (IPA, 99.5%) were purchased from Merck. Citric acid (99.5%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 98%), tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris, >99%), dopamine hydrochloride (>98%), polyethylenimine (PEI, Mw = 600 Da), ammonium persulfate (AP, >97%), N-(3-(dimethylamino)propyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, >99.0%), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, >99.0%), ethylene glycol (EG, >99.5%), and polyethylene glycols (PEGs) with different molecular weights (Mw = 400, 600, 1000, and 2000 Da) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) was purchased from Thermo Fischer Scientific. Crude oil (Heavy Aromatic-NaphthenicAzeri) was supplied by ONTA. De-ionized (DI) water used in this study was supplied by a Milli-Q unit (MilliPore) with a resistivity of 18 MΩcm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of tri-bore PVDF hollow fiber membranes
ce:section.ce:para: A series of tri-bore PVDF hollow fiber membranes were prepared via a dry-jet wet phase inversion spinning process using a custom-designed tri-bore spinneret (Fig. 1 a). The detailed spinning conditions were summarized in Table S1. Then, the as-spun membranes were soaked in tap water with daily change of water for 3 days to remove the residual solvent and non-solvent additives. Last, the fibers were freeze dried in a freeze dryer (S61-Modulyo-D, Thermo Electron Corp.) for 24 h for future use.

Na+-CQDs were synthesized following the procedure from previous studies [31,32]. First, citric acid powders were heated at 180 °C for 3 h. During the heating process, the citric acid was incompletely carbonized by losing some hydroxide groups, and the resultant CQDs had some carboxyl groups. They were then dissolved in DI water, and the solution was dialyzed against DI water using a Dialysis Cassette (Slide-A-Lyzer G2, 2 K MWCO). After the dialysis, the residue was neutralized to pH = 9 using a 5.0 M NaCl solution, during which the carboxyl groups of CQDs reacted with the hydroxide groups to produce CQDs containing sodium carboxylate (i.e., Na+-CQDs). The morphology of the synthesized Na+-CQDs was observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F), and their particle size distribution was obtained by counting 150 nanoparticles with the aid of ImageJ software (Fig. 1b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of Janus tri-bore hollow fiber membranes consisting of hydrophobic micropores and hydrophilic nanopores
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1c illustrates the scheme to fabricate a Janus membrane with hydrophobic micropores and hydrophilic nanopores. Dopamine hydrochloride (2 g/L), PEI (2 g/L), and AP (with a molar ratio of dopamine hydrochloride to AP = 2:1) were dissolved in a Tris buffer solution (pH = 8.5, 10 mM). Subsequently, a PVDF hollow fiber membrane, as the substrate, was immersed into the as-prepared dopamine/PEI solution for a certain duration. During deposition, the solution was kept at 60 °C. After the PDA/PEI deposition, the hollow fiber was rinsed by DI water and then assembled into a membrane module. Each module contains one fiber with an effective length of 12.5 cm. Hereafter, the PDA/PEI modified membrane was named as the PVDF-Px where the “x” was the deposition duration (h). In this study, PVDF-P0.5 and PVDF-P1 membranes were prepared.

Subsequently, the synthesized Na+-CQDs were covalently grafted on the PVDF-Px membrane surface. Specifically, a Na+-CQD solution (Na+-CQD: 10 g/L, EDC: 0.2 g/L, and NHS: 0.6 g/L) was circulated at the shell side of hollow fiber membrane modules at 40 °C for 24 h. With the aid of EDC and NHS, the carboxylate ions of Na+-CQDs could form covalent bonds with the NH2 groups of dopamine and PEI [33]. Afterwards, the membrane was rinsed with DI water to remove unbonded Na+-CQDs, and the final membrane was named as the PVDF-Px-CQD.

Mechanical properties of PVDF hollow fibers were determined by an Instron tensiometer (Model 5542, Instron Corp.). The starting gauge length and elongation rate were set at 50 mm and 10 mm/min, respectively. The liquid entry pressure (LEP) of PVDF hollow fiber membranes was measured using a method described in Supplementary Material. The membrane morphology was observed by a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, JEOL JSM-6700LV). The microstructure near the outer membrane surface was investigated by positron annihilation spectroscopy (PAS) coupled with Doppler broadening energy spectroscopy (DBES), using a 22Na slow positron beam source and a high-purity Ge detector. The mean implantation depth of positron into the membrane surface can be calculated from the incident energy, as given in Eq. (1) [34]:
(1)
Z
E +
=
40 ρ
E
+
1.6

where
Z
(nm) is the mean implantation depth,
ρ
(g/cm3) is the density of the polymer, which is 1.52 g/cm3 according to the literature [35], and
E +
(keV) is the positron incident energy.

The pore size distribution of the PVDF substrate was characterized by a capillary flow porometer based on a wet-up/dry-down method (CFP-1500AE, Porous Materials Inc), while the pore size distribution and molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of the PDA/PEI modified membranes were determined from solute transport tests (see the Supplementary Material for details). The surface chemistry of membranes was analyzed by attenuated total reflection Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR, Brucker) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos Analytical Ltd). The surface wettability of membranes was evaluated by measuring the in-air water contact angle (WCA). Before WCA measurements, hollow fiber membranes were flatten. Then, a water droplet of 1 µL was placed on the flattened fibers, and the WCA was analyzed by an optical system (DataPhysic, OCA25) using the sessile drop method.

DCMD wetting and fouling experiments were conducted using a laboratory setup as depicted in Fig. S3. For both wetting and fouling experiments, the feed solution was circulated along the shell side at a flow rate of 0.3 L min−1, and the distillate solution was circulated along the lumen side at a flow rate of 0.1 L min−1. The temperatures of the feed and distillate were maintained at 60 and 20 °C, respectively. During DCMD operations, the weight and conductivity of the distillate solution were continuously monitored to obtain the real-time vapor fluxes and salt rejections of membranes. The membrane flux was calculated by Eq. (2).
(2)
J =
Δ W
A × t

where
Δ W
(kg) is the mass change of the distillate solution over a period of
t
(h).
A
(m2) is the effective contacting area between the fiber and the feed solution. The salt rejection of a membrane was determined by Eq. (3).
(3)
R =
1 -
C d
C f
× 100 %

where
C f
(M) and
C d
(M) are the salt concentrations of the feed and distillate solutions, respectively. Considering the dilution effect, the distillate salt concentration was obtained using Eq. (4).
(4)
C d
=
C 1
m 1
-
C 0
m 0
m 1
-
m 0

where
m 0
(kg) and
m 1
(kg) are the initial and final weights of the solution in the distillate tank, respectively.
C 0
(M) and
C 1
(M) are the initial and final salt concentrations of the solution in the distillate tank. Both were determined from the solution conductivity.

In DCMD wetting experiments, a 0.6 M NaCl solution and DI water were initially used as the feed and distillate solutions, respectively. After 1-h operation, SDS was regularly added into the feed solution every 2 h; each addition increased the SDS concentration of 0.1 mM. The maximum SDS concentration in wetting experiments was 0.4 mM because the surface tension of a 0.6 M NaCl solution does not decrease further beyond this concentration [29].

In DCMD fouling experiments, a saline oil-in-water emulsion and DI water were circulated as the feed and distillate solutions, respectively. The saline oil-in-water emulsion (0.6 M NaCl, 1 g/L crude oil, and 0.03 g/L SDS) was thoroughly mixed by a homogenizer (GLH850, Omni) at 20000 rpm for 15 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Desalination of a simulated multi-component wastewater
ce:section.ce:para: Real wastewater is a complex matrix containing multiple contaminants such as inorganic salts, organic compounds, crude oil, and surfactants [19,36,37]. Therefore, a simulated multi-component wastewater was employed as the feed in DCMD experiments to evaluate the practical significance of the newly developed membrane. The pristine PVDF membrane was also tested for comparison. The composition of the simulated wastewater is given in Table S2. Wastewater desalination experiments were terminated when the membrane flux decreased to zero or the water recovery reached 50% (i.e., the initial feed volume was 1L). Other experimental conditions kept identical with those in DCMD wetting and fouling experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties of tri-bore PVDF hollow fiber membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Table 1 compares the physical properties of tri-bore PVDF hollow fiber membranes, including dimensions, mechanical properties, LEPs, and MD fluxes, as a function of spinning conditions. Among them, Tri-A displays the highest mechanical strength, the highest LEP, and the lowest membrane flux due to a higher PVDF concentration of 12 wt% employed during the spinning. In addition, the external coagulant composition also affects the membrane properties. At the same PVDF concentration of 10 wt%, the MD flux increases while the LEP and mechanical strength decrease with an increase in IPA concentration in the external coagulation bath. The variations of flux, LEP, and mechanical strength can be attributed to the use of a weak coagulant (i.e., IPA/water mixture) that not only delays the liquid–liquid demixing during phase inversion but also increases both surface porosity and surface pore size (Fig. S4b-d) [8,38]. On the other hand, the delayed liquid–liquid demixing promotes the solid–liquid demixing and results in a macrovoids-free structure near the outer surface (Fig. S4j-l) [39]. Since the replacement of macrovoids in the cross-section by the cellular structure increases the resistance for vapor transport [14], the flux enhancements by using weak external coagulants are not significant. Therefore, to achieve a high MD flux while maintaining high LEP and mechanical properties, as well as to minimize the use of organic solvents in membrane fabrication, the Tri-B membrane is selected as the substrate to fabricate the Janus MD membranes. It is also referred to as the pristine PVDF membrane in the following discussion. Comparing to single-bore hollow fiber membranes, the tri-bore PVDF hollow fiber substrate displays superior mechanical strength owing to the spokes-like structure that not only restrains the radical deformation but also dissipates the axial load (Table S3) [14,15].

The pristine PVDF membrane displays a porous outer surface (Fig. 2 a). Compared to the pristine PVDF membrane, the outer surfaces of the PVDF-P0.5 and PVDF-P1 are both thoroughly covered by the aggregates of PDA/PEI polymers. A comparison of Fig. 2b and c indicates a longer deposition duration results in more polymer aggregates. On the other hand, the cross-sectional structure of the PVDF substrate is not changed after the PDA/PEI deposition because its hydrophobicity prevents the dopamine/PEI solution from intruding into the substrate (Fig. 2e, f and g). Therefore, the PDA/PEI deposition is only applied to the outer surface of the substrate. Interestingly, the Na+-CQD grafting does not lead to a significant change in surface morphology because the ultrafine Na+-CQD nanoparticles with an average diameter of 3.58 nm cannot be directly observed under FESEM observation (Fig. 2d and Fig. 1b). However, the existence of Na+-CQDs on membrane surface can be confirmed by FTIR and XPS, and the results will be discussed later.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thickness and pore size of the PDA/PEI layer
ce:section.ce:para: The R parameter measured by DBES, as a function of positron incident energy (bottom X axis) and depth into a membrane (top X axis), was employed to examine the microstructure of membranes with different PDA/PEI depositions (Fig. 3 a). The R parameter qualitatively reflects the intensity of nanometer voids within the membrane. Generally, a larger R theoretically corresponds to a larger pore size [40,41]. For both PVDF-P0.5 and PVDF-P1 membranes, their R parameters first decrease and then increase, which suggests a dense-to-porous transition from the PDA/PEI layer to the PVDF substrate [42]. Therefore, the distance between the initial point and the lowest point in the R parameter curve can be interpretated as the thickness of the PDA/PEI layer. Accordingly, the thicknesses of the PDA/PEI layers of PVDF-P0.5 and PVDF-P1 membranes are about ∼ 45 and ∼ 152 nm, respectively. In addition, compared to the PVDF-P0.5 membrane, the lower R parameter of the PVDF-P1 membrane implies that a longer deposition duration not only increases the layer thickness but also decreases the pore size of the PDA/PEI layer.

Pore sizes of the pristine and PDA/PEI modified membranes were also measured. The pristine PVDF hollow fiber membrane possesses an average pore size of 0.409 µm (Fig. 3b), which is consistent with conventional hydrophobic MD membranes [43]. The introduction of a PDA/PEI layer on the substrate drastically decreases the membrane pore size. With a 0.5 h PDA/PEI deposition, the membrane has an average pore size of 1.102 nm and a MWCO of 1915 Da (Fig. 3c). A longer deposition duration of 1 h further reduces the pore size to 0.758 nm and the MWCO to 706 Da (Fig. 3d). These results indicate that the PDA/PEI layers are nanoporous, resembling the typical selective layers of NF membranes which have pore diameters less than 2 nm and MWCOs of 150–2000 Da [44,45]. The significant decline in pore size is also evidenced by the appreciable drop in gas permeance of membranes (Fig. S5a). The deviation of O2/N2 selectivity from the Knudsen diffusion selectivity also confirms that the PDA/PEI layers have smaller pores than the Knudsen diffusion regime of 2–50 nm (Fig. S5b) [46]. Generally, pure PDA films with a loose structure could act as the selective layer for NF, but post treatments such as crosslinking and mineralization are necessary [47–50]. However, the reaction between PDA and PEI inhibits the formation of large PDA aggregates and results in a dense PDA/PEI layer. In this study, AP is used as a catalyst. Compared to conventional air-oxidized PDA/PEI depositions, the presence of AP significantly reduces the deposition duration because it facilitates the rapid and homogeneous oxidation of dopamine in the solution [51,52].

Fig. 4 a compares the surface chemistry of membranes analyzed by ATR-FTIR. Comparing to the pristine PVDF membrane, the PVDF-P0.5 and PVDF-P1 membranes display a characteristic peak of C = N vibration at 1620 cm−1 arising from the Schiff-base reaction between PDA and PEI [53,54]. The other new absorption peak at 3000–3600 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of –NH and –OH groups [55,56]. For the PVDF-P1-CQD membrane, two new absorption peaks corresponding to the asymmetric COO– stretching (1571 cm−1) and the C = O stretching in –COOH groups (1710 cm−1) are observed [31,57]. These verify the successful grafting of Na+-CQDs on the membrane surface.

The outer surface chemistry analyzed by XPS is illustrated in Fig. 4b. As expected, C and F elements are the predominant elements of the pristine PVDF membrane. The existence of the O 1 s peak is probably due to the absorbed moisture and oxygen. After the PDA/PEI deposition, a new peak of N 1 s arises from the PVDF-P0.5 and PVDF-P1 membrane surfaces, and its content increases with the deposition duration. Since the thickness of the PDA/PEI layer is in a range of tens to hundreds of nanometers (Fig. 3a), the disappearance of the F 1 s peak is attributed to the full coverage of the PDA/PEI layer on the PVDF substrate. After the Na+-CQD grafting on the membrane, a comparison between PVDF-P1 and PVDF-P1-CQD XPS spectra indicates a significant increase in O content from 20.41 to 30.33% because Na+-CQDs have abundant oxygen-containing functional groups.

Various surface characteristics confer different surface wettability to the membranes. As shown in Fig. 4c, the pristine PVDF membrane is relatively hydrophobic with a high WCA of 81.03°. In contrast, the PDA/PEI modified membranes are hydrophilic due to the hydrophilic nature of PDA and PEI. Their WCAs decrease with an increase in the duration for the PDA/PEI deposition. Specifically, the deposition durations of 0.5 h and 1 h result in WCA values of 44.67° and 32.37°, respectively. Despite no chemical bonds between the PDA/PEI layer and the PVDF substrate, the hydrophilic modification by the PDA/PEI deposition is irreversible and stable. The WCAs of PVDF-P0.5 and PVDF-P1 membranes remain smaller than 55° and 40°, respectively, after 30-min ultrasonication (180 W, HCS Scientific & Chemical, Singapore) (Fig. S6). After the Na+-CQD grafting, the PVDF-P1-CQD membrane displays excellent hydrophilicity. It has an extremely low WCA of 7.7° due to the enhanced O content on the membrane surface.

Maintaining membranes in a non-wetted status is of critical importance for sustainable MD operations. In DCMD wetting experiments, we challenged the pristine and PDA/PEI modified membranes using SDS as the wetting agent. For the pristine PVDF membrane, wetting phenomenon is evidenced by the drops in both salt rejection and vapor flux after the first SDS addition (Fig. 5 a). The mechanism of surfactant-induced wetting is schematically illustrated in Fig. 5d. Since the SDS addition effectively reduces the overall surface tension of the feed solution and thus the LEP value, the intrusion of the saline feed into membrane pores is facilitated [19]. Meanwhile, SDS molecules readily adsorb and align on the pore surface due to the hydrophobic-hydrophobic interaction between the surfactant and the hydrophobic membrane. This leads to expose their hydrophilic heads toward the feed [58]. Consequently, membrane wetting occurs because these exposed hydrophilic heads assist the saline feed to pass through membrane pores easily.

In comparison, the deposit of a PDA/PEI layer on top of the PVDF membrane significantly reduces the wetting propensity. The PVDF-P0.5 membrane displays a slight wetting phenomenon in the experiment. Both vapor flux and salt rejection show signs of decline when the SDS concentration is increased to 0.2 mM. They finally drop to 74% and 98. 7% respectively at the end of the experiment (Fig. 5b). However, wetting is not observable when the PDA/PEI deposition duration is extended to 1 h. The PVDF-P1 membrane displays robust MD performance throughout the entire wetting experiment. It has a relatively stable vapor flux and a perfect salt rejection even at the SDS concentration of 0.4 mM (Fig. 5c). Clearly, the nanoporous PDA/PEI layer endows the PVDF-P0.5 and PVDF-P1 membranes with better anti-wetting properties by rejecting SDS via size exclusion, as illustrated in Fig. 5d. In addition, since the high salinity of the feed (i.e., 0.6 M NaCl) should shield the electrostatic interaction between the surfactant and membrane surface, this let size exclusion to be the dominant role in rejecting the surfactant [19,29,58].

Based on the molecule modelling by Material Studio, an SDS molecule has a size of ∼ 0.7 × 2 nm2. Its size would be larger when its hydrophilic head is hydrated in water. In addition, the amphiphilic property of SDS molecules determines if they would either form aggregates or micelles with a diameter of 3.5–4 nm at high concentrations [59,60]. Since the PDA/PEI layers are nanoporous and have pore sizes similar to the typical selective layers of NF membranes (Fig. 3c and d), they should be able to reject SDS molecules, aggregates, and micelles, while allowing the passage of water (0.28 nm) and salt [Na+ (0.72 nm) and Cl- (0.66 nm)]. After removing the surfactant from the feed, the remaining saline solution in contact with the interface between the hydrophilic PDA/PEI layer and the hydrophobic PVDF substrate has a higher surface tension. Thus, wetting is mitigated. However, the PVDF-P0.5 membrane does not exhibit a satisfactory SDS rejection due to its relatively large pore size and wide pore size distribution (Fig. 3c). Therefore, it cannot not completely prevent wetting in the experiment (Fig. 5b). On the other hand, once the PDA/PEI deposition duration is extended to 1 h, the PVDF-P1 membrane has a high SDS rejection because it not only has a small pore size and MWCO, but also a sharp pore size distribution (Fig. 3d). Thus, it is able to prevent wetting occurrence in the wetting experiment (Fig. 5c). This excellent wetting resistance is further evidenced in a 100 h long-term DCMD test where the PVDF-P1 membrane is faced with a feed consisting of 0.6 M NaCl and 0.1 mM SDS (Fig. S7). This surfactant concentration is often detected in municipal wastewaters (0.01–0.02 g/L) [61,62].

Generally, the gas permeability of membranes is used as an indicator of transport resistance in an MD process [26,63]. Interestingly, in this study, the gas permeance of membranes decreases by 2 orders of magnitude after the PDA/PEI deposition (Fig. S5a), while the initial MD fluxes of the PVDF, PVDF-P0.5, PVDF-P1 membranes are 18.3, 15.9 and 12.4 Lm-2h−1, respectively, all of which are in the same order. This phenomenon can be explained by the distinct transport behaviors between gases (i.e., O2 and N2) and water vapor within the PDA/PEI layer. For O2 and N2, the gases flow through the pores directly without interacting with the PDA/PEI layer. Thus, the drastic decline in pore size after the PDA/PEI deposition leads to significant decreases in O2 and N2 permeance. In contrast, water is immediately absorbed in the hydrophilic PDA/PEI layer during the DCMD operation due to the capillary force, and then water vapor is generated at the hydrophilic-hydrophobic (i.e., PDA/PEI-PVDF) interface. Consequently, the water vapor transport is mainly determined by the temperature at the interface and the properties (e.g., pore size and wettability) of the PVDF hydrophobic substrate. Since the PVDF, PVDF-P0.5, PVDF-P1 tri-bore hollow fiber membranes have almost the same substrate structure while the nanopores in the PDA/PEI layers hinder the effective mixing of the feed saline solution, this would exacerbate the temperature polarization in the PDA/PEI layers and undermine the overall driving force [21,58]. Thus, the order of the initial MD fluxes follows PVDF > PVDF-P0.5 > PVDF-P1.

Given the satisfactory anti-wetting performance of the PVDF-P1 membrane, it is further grafted by Na+-CQDs for fouling tests. Fig. 6 compares the the fouling resistance of the PVDF, PVDF-P1, and PVDF-P1-CQD membranes using a crude oil as the model organic foulant. The pristine PVDF membrane is immediately fouled from the onset of the fouling experiment where the vapor flux decreases to almost zero within 2 h (Fig. 6a). As illustrated in Fig. 6d, the rapid fouling is attributed to the attractive hydrophobic-hydrophobic interaction between the PVDF membrane surface and oil, under which oil droplets are glued to the membrane surface [21]. The accumulated oil blocks membrane pores and eliminates the channels for vapor transport, leading to a severe flux decline.

In comparison, Janus membranes with a hydrophilic surface show enhanced resistance against oil fouling. The PVDF-P1 membrane displays a slower flux decline as its flux drops to almost zero after 8 h (Fig. 6b). Interestingly, no significant flux decline is observed for the PVDF-P1-CQD membrane during the entire 12-h fouling experiment (Fig. 6c). The enhanced oil fouling resistance arises from the hydrophilic surface modifications by both PDA/PEI deposition and Na+-CQD grafting, which facilitate the formation of a hydration layer on the membrane surface (Fig. 6d). Generally, the PDA/PEI deposition not only reduces the pore size but also introduces hydrophilic moieties such as –NH2 and –OH groups on the membrane surface. These hydrophilic moieties can form hydrogen bonds with water as a hydration layer on the surface and retard the oil attachment on the membrane surface. Since oil-induced fouling involves destroying the hydration layer, it is energetically unfavorable [19,64,65]. Nonetheless, a mere PDA/PEI deposition does not ensure excellent anti-fouling property due to the limited hydrophilicity. The subsequent Na+-CQD grafting delivers improved surface hydrophilicity by increasing the oxygen content at the membrane surface (Fig. 4b and c). Therefore, the PVDF-P1-CQD membrane is underwater-oleophobic and has a stronger oil-fouling resistance because a more robust hydration layer is formed on its Na+-CQDs grafted layer. The surface morphology of membranes after fouling experiments can be found in Fig. S8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Desalination performance with a simulated wastewater
ce:section.ce:para: We further test the desalination performance of the PVDF and PVDF-P1-CQD membranes using a simulated multi-component wastewater as the feed in DCMD experiments. With the recovery of water from the feed solution, the contaminants concentration keeps increasing during the experiments. To facilitate a meaningful comparison between membranes with different initial fluxes, desalination performance (i.e., normalized flux and distillate conductivity) of membranes is presented as a function of water recovery (Fig. 7 ).

As shown in Fig. 7, the pristine PVDF membrane experiences a vapor flux decline upon the onset of experiment and finally achieves a limiting water recovery of ∼ 27.5%. This is attributed to membrane fouling because organic foulants, including humic acid and oil, are prone to attach on the hydrophobic membrane surface. Meanwhile, the sharp increase in distillate conductivity indicates the occurrence of pore wetting that is likely induced by surfactant or inorganic scales. In contrast, no significant performance deterioration is observed for the PVDF-P1-CQD membrane. A slight flux decline and a negligible distillate conductivity increase are detected even when the feed solution is concentrated 2-fold. Our results suggest that the newly developed membrane can simultaneously mitigate organic fouling and prevent surfactant-induced wetting, further proving its practical application value in treating real complex wastewaters.

In summary, to overcome the issues of weak mechanical properties, membrane wetting and fouling in MD operations, we have developed a robust tri-bore Janus hollow fiber membrane consisting of 3 layers; namely, a porous and hydrophobic tri-bore PVDF hollow fiber substrate, an oxidant-induced hydrophilic PDA/PEI layer, and a highly hydrophilic Na+-CQD grafting layer. Its wetting and fouling resistance properties were characterized using saline feed solutions containing a crude oil and various SDS concentrations. It is found that the optimal PDA/PEI deposition duration of 1 h endows the membrane with sufficient resistance against surfactant-induced wetting via size exclusion, while the highly hydrophilic Na+-CQD grafting layer efficiently mitigates the fouling via the formation of a hydration layer that deters the oil attachment. With excellent wetting and fouling resistance, the newly developed membrane displayed robust performance when desalinating a multi-component wastewater. This study may provide a new window to molecularly design MD membranes with high mechanical strength and simultaneous wetting and fouling resistance.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Yuanmiaoliang Chen is grateful to the National University of Singapore Graduate School for Integrative Sciences & Engineering Scholarship (NGSS). Special thanks to Mr. Chuning Shang for the PAS measurements and Dr. Wenxiao Gai for the CQD synthesis. Prof Chung also likes to thank the Yushan Scholar Program supported by the Ministry of Education, Taiwan.
